description: GKG webserver dashboard. Top rows tell the latency story: a Rails request lands on GKG over gRPC, the pipeline runs (authorize → compile → execute → hydrate), and Rails redacts before the response goes out. Bottom rows are reliability, resources, and the per-domain catalog reference (collapsed by default).

panels.description: p95 of the user-facing Rails-side gRPC call to GKG over the last 5 minutes. This is the latency the GitLab.com web request actually experiences.
panels.title: Rails: gRPC p95 (5m)

panels.description: Share of query pipeline calls with status="ok" over the last hour. Renders 0 when no calls happened in the window.
panels.title: Pipeline: success rate (1h)

panels.description: p95 of overall pipeline duration on the GKG webserver side. Compared with the Rails p95 above, the difference is network + JWT/auth + redaction.
panels.title: Pipeline: p95 (5m)

panels.description: Pipeline calls rejected for security policy violations. Includes rate-limit, depth, validation, allowlist, auth-filter, and timeout rejects.

targets.expr: (sum(rate(gkg_query_engine_threat_allowlist_rejected_total{container="gkg-webserver", cluster=~"$cluster"}[5m]) + rate(gkg_query_engine_threat_auth_filter_missing_total{container="gkg-webserver", cluster=~"$cluster"}[5m]) + rate(gkg_query_engine_threat_depth_exceeded_total{container="gkg-webserver", cluster=~"$cluster"}[5m]) + rate(gkg_query_engine_threat_limit_exceeded_total{container="gkg-webserver", cluster=~"$cluster"}[5m]) + rate(gkg_query_engine_threat_rate_limited_total{container="gkg-webserver", cluster=~"$cluster"}[5m]) + rate(gkg_query_engine_threat_timeout_total{container="gkg-webserver", cluster=~"$cluster"}[5m]) + rate(gkg_query_engine_threat_validation_failed_total{container="gkg-webserver", cluster=~"$cluster"}[5m])) * 60) or vector(0)

panels.title: Threat rejects / min (5m)

panels.description: Query pipeline calls on the GKG webserver in the dashboard window. Click the arrow to open the per-status rate in Explore.
panels.title: Queries (server)

panels.description: Rails-side gRPC calls into GKG in the dashboard window. Should track closely with server-side queries; a divergence flags either a Rails-side retry storm or server-side rejected requests not counted on Rails.
panels.title: gRPC calls (Rails)

panels.description: Rails-side redaction passes in the dashboard window. Roughly one per successful query.
panels.title: Redactions (Rails)

panels.description: ClickHouse row-read events in the dashboard window. Bytes read tile sits next to it.
panels.title: CH row reads

panels.description: Rate of change over time. Hover for exact values; click the link in the header for the full Explore view.
Rate of change over time. Hover for exact values; click the link in the header for the full Explore view.
Rate of change over time. Hover for exact values; click the link in the header for the full Explore view.
Rate of change over time. Hover for exact values; click the link in the header for the full Explore view.

panels.title: Latency story (Rails → Server → ClickHouse → Rails redaction)

panels.description: p95 of the Rails-side gRPC call to GKG, broken down by RPC method. This is the user-visible latency. Use it as the ceiling — every other panel in this row should sit underneath it.
panels.title: Rails: gRPC duration p95 by method (5m)

panels.description: GKG-side query pipeline duration percentiles. The gap between this and the Rails p95 above is wire time + JWT/auth-context build + redaction.
panels.title: Server: pipeline duration (p50/p95/p99)

panels.description: p95 per pipeline stage stacked together. The visual height tells you which stage is eating the budget on a slow request — compile, authorization, execute, or hydration.
panels.title: Server: pipeline stages p95 (stacked)

panels.description: p50/p95/p99 of the pipeline execute stage — the time spent waiting on ClickHouse. Execute spike + flat compile/hydrate means a slow query plan, not server overhead.
panels.title: Server: ClickHouse execute (p50/p95/p99)

panels.description: p50/p95/p99 of the hydration stage — graph response shaping after ClickHouse returns. Hydration spike + flat execute means the row count is huge; cross-check the result-set rows panel below.
panels.title: Server: hydration (p50/p95/p99)

panels.description: p50/p95/p99 of redaction time on the Rails side, after GKG returns. Adds onto the server pipeline duration to make the total the user sees.
panels.title: Rails: redaction duration p50/p95/p99 (5m)

panels.description: p95 of how many rows redaction sees per request, and how many it filters out. Big batch + low filtered count is fine; small batch + everything filtered is the worst case for the user (lots of work, nothing returned).
panels.title: Rails: redaction batch size and filtered rows (p95)

panels.description: Rails-observed gRPC p95 plotted next to the server pipeline p95. The gap is the round-trip wire time plus JWT, auth-context, and redaction. A growing gap with stable server p95 means the slowdown is on the Rails side.
panels.title: Rails total p95 vs Server pipeline p95

panels.description: Bytes and rows pulled from ClickHouse per second. Lets you cross-correlate execute-stage latency with the volume of work the query actually did.
panels.title: ClickHouse: bytes / rows read per second

panels.description: p50/p95/p99 of ClickHouse memory_usage reported per query. A jump here often correlates with execute-stage latency and is the first signal of a query plan regression.
panels.title: ClickHouse: memory_usage_bytes per query (p50/p95/p99)

panels.description: Time spent on Rails preparing the JWT and the auth-context to attach to the gRPC call. Climbs here mean the Rails-side request setup is the bottleneck, not GKG.
panels.title: Rails: JWT build and auth-context p95 (5m)

panels.description: p50/p95/p99 of how many rows each query returned. A latency climb that tracks a row-count climb is just more work; a climb that does not is a regression.
panels.title: Server: result-set rows per query (p50/p95/p99)

panels.description: GKG-side pipeline error counts in rolling 1h windows, one series per error kind. Lets you tell apart authorization failures, content resolution failures, execution failures, security rejections, and streaming failures.
panels.title: Server: pipeline errors by kind (1h windows)

panels.description: Rails-side gRPC error counts in rolling 1h windows, by gRPC status code. Auth or unavailable spikes here often line up with the server reliability panel on the left.
panels.title: Rails: gRPC errors by code (1h windows)

panels.description: Threat-policy rejections grouped by reason. These are not application errors — they are intentional rejections for rate, depth, validation, allowlist, missing-auth-filter, timeout, or validation issues.
panels.title: Server: threat counters by kind (1h windows)

panels.description: Current count of active gRPC requests on the GKG webserver, summed across pods. A flat ceiling here is the saturation signal for the gRPC server.
panels.title: Server: in-flight gRPC requests

panels.description: Number of traversal IDs Rails attaches to each gRPC call (pre-compaction). p95 climbing means Rails is sending bigger authorization payloads.
panels.title: Rails: traversal IDs per request (p50/p95)

panels.description: Ratio of compacted size to original. Lower is better — 0.1 means the compaction reduced the payload to 10% of original. Watch for sustained 1.0 (no benefit).
panels.title: Rails: compaction ratio (p50/p95)

panels.description: Rate of requests where compaction fell back to the uncompressed form (left) or where the traversal ID list exceeded the threshold (right). Rising fallback often precedes a tail-latency event on the Rails side.
panels.title: Rails: compaction fallback / threshold-exceeded per minute

panels.description: Time to resolve content for a request via Gitaly. Spikes here usually correspond with execute-stage latency on queries that touch blob content.
panels.title: Resolve duration (p50/p95/p99)

panels.description: Number of items per content-resolve batch. Useful for telling apart "many small calls" from "one fat call" when interpreting the duration panel.
panels.title: Resolve batch size (p50/p95/p99)

panels.description: Total list_blobs RPCs issued to Gitaly.
panels.title: gkg_content_gitaly_calls_total — rate/s

panels.description: Webserver readiness gate state; 1 indicates the active state per `state` label.
panels.title: gkg_webserver_schema_state

panels.description: Aggregate CPU seconds used per second divided by configured CPU limit, across all webserver replicas. 1.0 means the pool is fully consuming its quota.

targets.expr: ((sum(rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{container="gkg-webserver", cluster=~"$cluster", cpu="total"}[5m]))) / (sum(kube_pod_container_resource_limits{container="gkg-webserver", cluster=~"$cluster", namespace="gkg", resource="cpu"}) > 0)) or vector(0)

panels.title: CPU: usage / limit (5m)

panels.description: Working-set memory across all replicas as a fraction of the aggregate memory limit. Watch for sustained values above ~0.85 — that is OOM-kill territory.

targets.expr: ((sum(container_memory_working_set_bytes{container="gkg-webserver", cluster=~"$cluster"})) / (sum(kube_pod_container_resource_limits{container="gkg-webserver", cluster=~"$cluster", namespace="gkg", resource="memory"}) > 0)) or vector(0)

panels.title: Memory: working set / limit

panels.description: Total OOM-killer events across webserver replicas in the last hour. Any non-zero value warrants a look at the memory panel below.
panels.title: OOM events (1h)

panels.description: Container restarts across webserver replicas in the last hour. Crash-loops show up here before they show up in the app metrics.
panels.title: Restarts (1h)

panels.description: Per-pod CPU seconds consumed per second. The dashed reference line is the per-pod CPU limit from kube-state-metrics; pods bumping against it should be cross-checked with the throttling panel.
panels.title: CPU: cores used per pod

panels.description: Share of CFS periods where the cgroup was throttled. Anything sustained above ~5% means the pod is hitting its CPU quota and requests will queue.
panels.title: CPU: throttled time fraction per pod

panels.description: container_memory_working_set_bytes per replica. The dashed reference line is the per-pod memory limit. Working set is what the OOM killer reads, not RSS.
panels.title: Memory: working set per pod

panels.description: Resident set and page cache, per pod. RSS dominating with a tiny cache often means heap growth; cache dominating typically means heavy I/O.
panels.title: Memory: RSS and cache per pod

panels.description: Read and write throughput from cAdvisor. Sustained climbs here on the webserver usually point at log volume or temp-file churn.
panels.title: Filesystem I/O bytes per pod

panels.description: PSI seconds per second. IO pressure rising means processes are waiting on disk; memory pressure rising means the kernel is reclaiming pages, often the leading edge of an OOM.
panels.title: Pressure stall: IO and memory

panels.description: OS-level concurrency counters. Threads climbing without a corresponding workload increase usually points at a tokio blocking-pool growth event; sockets climbing flags a connection leak.
panels.title: Threads and sockets per pod

panels.description: HTTP server request latency (autoinstrumentation).

targets.expr: histogram_quantile(0.5, sum by (le, http_route, http_response_status_code, http_request_method) (rate(http_server_request_duration_seconds_bucket{container="gkg-webserver", cluster=~"$cluster"}[5m])))

histogram_quantile(0.95, sum by (le, http_route, http_response_status_code, http_request_method) (rate(http_server_request_duration_seconds_bucket{container="gkg-webserver", cluster=~"$cluster"}[5m])))

histogram_quantile(0.99, sum by (le, http_route, http_response_status_code, http_request_method) (rate(http_server_request_duration_seconds_bucket{container="gkg-webserver", cluster=~"$cluster"}[5m])))

panels.title: http_server_request_duration_seconds — p50/p95/p99

title: Orbit — GKG webserver
